subject: Civics
title: Parliament and the Making of Laws

questions.text: Why should people decide in a democracy?
options: Because a democracy is built on the participation and consent of the people
questions.explanation: In a democracy, the people are the ultimate source of power and their 'consent' is vital.

questions.text: What was the main experience of Indians under British rule that shaped their desire for democracy?
options: Living in fear and not being able to criticize government decisions
questions.explanation: The colonial experience made Indians value the freedom to participate in decision-making.

questions.text: The individual or citizen is the most important person in which form of government?
questions.explanation: In principle, the government as well as other public institutions need to have the trust of the citizens.

questions.text: How do people give 'consent' to the government?
questions.explanation: People elect their representatives to the Parliament, and one group from these representatives forms the government.

questions.text: What is the 'Sansad' in India?
questions.explanation: The Parliament of India (Sansad) is the supreme law-making institution.

questions.text: The Parliament of India consists of:
options: President, Rajya Sabha and Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: The Parliament includes the President and the two houses.

questions.text: Which house is also known as the 'Council of States'?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha represents the states of India in the Parliament.

questions.text: Which house is also known as the 'House of the People'?
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha is directly elected by the people of India.

questions.text: What is the maximum number of members in the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The maximum strength is 250, with 245 current members (including 12 nominated by the President).

questions.text: How many members are directly elected to the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The country is divided into 543 constituencies for Lok Sabha elections.

questions.text: Who is the 'Executive' head that the Lok Sabha selects?
questions.explanation: The PM is the leader of the majority party in the Lok Sabha and heads the executive.

questions.text: A political party needs at least how many seats to have a majority in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: Half of 543 is 271.5, so at least 272 members are needed for a majority.

questions.text: What is a 'Coalition Government'?
options: Government formed by several parties joining together
questions.explanation: When no single party gets a majority, different parties join to form a coalition.

questions.text: Who chairs the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Vice-President is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

questions.text: What is the 'Question Hour' in Parliament?
options: Time to ask questions to ministers about government functioning
questions.explanation: The Question Hour is a mechanism through which MPs can elicit information and hold the government accountable.

questions.text: MPs who are not part of the ruling party or coalition form the ________.
questions.explanation: The opposition parties play a crucial role by highlighting drawbacks in government policies.

questions.text: How do laws get passed in Parliament?
options: They are introduced as bills and debated/voted on in both houses
questions.explanation: Parliament is the law-making body of the nation.

questions.text: Who are the people in the Parliament today?
options: From diverse backgrounds, including rural and marginalized groups
questions.explanation: Parliament has seen increasing participation from rural areas, Dalits, and Backward Castes.

questions.text: Why are some seats 'reserved' in Parliament for SC and ST candidates?
options: To give them a chance to participate in decision-making
To make the Parliament look full
questions.explanation: Reservation ensures that communities that have been historically marginalized are represented.

questions.text: What is the demand regarding women's representation in Parliament?
questions.explanation: There is a long-standing debate to reserve 33% of seats for women to improve their low representation.

questions.text: Elections to the Lok Sabha are held every ________ years.
questions.explanation: Both Lok Sabha and State Assemblies have a normal term of 5 years.

questions.text: Constituency refers to:
options: A specific area from which voters elect one representative
questions.explanation: For elections, the country/state is divided into many small areas called constituencies.

questions.text: The members of the Rajya Sabha are elected by:
options: Elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha election is an indirect election by MLAs.

questions.text: Can the Rajya Sabha initiate legislation?
options: Yes, but it must be passed by both houses
questions.explanation: A bill must pass through both houses to become law.

questions.text: The 'approval' of the people in a democracy is expressed through ________.
questions.explanation: Elections are the primary way citizens control and guide the government.

questions.text: Who is the leader of the ruling party in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister selects ministers from the party to work with him/her to implement decisions.

questions.text: What is the term of a member of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent house, but individual members have a term of 6 years (1/3rd retire every 2 years).

questions.text: The main function of the 'Opposition' is to:
options: Point out flaws in government policies
questions.explanation: A healthy opposition is essential for a functioning democracy to check the power of the ruling party.

questions.text: How many members are nominated to the Rajya Sabha by the President?
questions.explanation: 12 members are nominated for their contributions to art, literature, science, or social service.

questions.text: EVMS (Electronic Voting Machines) were used throughout the country for the first time in which general elections?
questions.explanation: The 2004 general elections were the first to use EVMs nationwide, saving about 1,50,000 trees.

questions.text: The Parliament is the expression of the ________ that people of India have in principles of democracy.
questions.explanation: It symbolizes the people's participation in decision-making.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a function of the Parliament?
options: To control and inform the government
To write the Constitution for the first time
questions.explanation: The original Constitution was written by the Constituent Assembly; the current Parliament can only amend it.

questions.text: Each constituency elects ________ person to the Parliament.
questions.explanation: Each constituency is represented by a single MP.

questions.text: Once elected, these candidates become ________.
questions.explanation: MPs (Members of Parliament) together make up the Sansad.

questions.text: The Rajya Sabha functions primarily as the ________.
options: Representative of the States of India
questions.explanation: Its role is to protect the interests and perspectives of the state governments in the center.

questions.text: Can a bill become a law if the Rajya Sabha rejects it?
options: No, it requires consent of both houses (with some exceptions like Money Bills)
Only if the President says so
Yes, if the PM is strong
questions.explanation: Most bills require both houses to agree. Disagreements may lead to a joint session.

questions.text: The Parliament 'controls' the government by:
questions.explanation: By asking questions, MPs alert the government to its shortcomings and the opinion of the public.

questions.text: In the 2019 Lok Sabha elections, which party won with a clear majority?
questions.explanation: The Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) won 303 seats, more than the required 272.

questions.text: Which of these is a major challenge for the Indian Parliament today?
questions.explanation: Women represent only a small percentage (around 14%) of the Parliament, which is considered inadequate.

questions.text: The most important role of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha is to:
options: Preside over the house and maintain order
questions.explanation: The Speaker ensures the house functions smoothly and according to the rules.
